description: A signed, chained, Merkle-verifiable audit trail. Every action gets one SHA3-256 hash, one Ed25519 signature, one chain link, one Merkle proof — and the whole thing verifies in any language with crypto primitives. No trusted audit service required.

records.summary: An agent was spawned. Real Ed25519 keypair, real SHA3-256 digest, real Merkle path against the chain root.
records.aftermath: The new agent's public key is added to the trust store and the genesis event hash becomes the anchor that every subsequent action chains off. Hot tier: in-memory log + write-ahead log. Anyone connecting later can ask for the genesis event and verify the public key matches what's expected.

beats.copy: An agent was spawned — event 1 of 5 in this trace. Every audit trail starts somewhere. This is the genesis event — no predecessor, just a public key and a starting hash.

Genesis event. There's no agent before this one — agent_id "agent-orchestrator" is being created. The payload (the spawn parameters) gets hashed to 0eb9efc80f67…; the spawn arguments themselves stay in the host system, not in the chain.

Encode to canonical bytes: 174 bytes. Domain tag PROV_EVENT_V1, schema version 1, length-prefixed strings, metadata sorted by key. Re-encoding the same event always produces these exact bytes — that's what "canonical" means.

SHA3-256 of the canonical bytes lands on 9c8aaa3c248546…. Ed25519 signs that hash with the agent's private key → 64-byte signature. The 32-byte public key gets attached so anyone can verify without contacting the signer.
Genesis event. prev_event_hash is null — there's no predecessor. The trace starts here, and every later event's chain link points back to this one (eventually).
Merkle proof: 3 sibling hashes (~96 bytes). Walking those siblings reconstructs the chain root (e38439a06313…). Proof size stays small even when the chain grows large.

Anyone with the agent's public key (0d49ff0d4743…) and the chain root (e38439a06313…) can verify: signature verifies ✓, chain link links cleanly ✓, Merkle proof verifies against the chain root ✓. Three independent checks, all local, all crypto. No "audit service" in the loop to trust.

records.summary: The agent read a file. Real Ed25519 keypair, real SHA3-256 digest, real Merkle path against the chain root.
records.aftermath: The read is logged with the file path + byte count, but the file contents are never in the chain — only their hash would be, if we needed to prove what the agent saw. For now the event tells the auditor: this agent, at this position in the trace, read this path. Nothing more, nothing less.

beats.copy: The agent read a file — event 2 of 5 in this trace. Every tool the agent runs becomes a signed event. The signature proves which agent did it; the chain link proves when, relative to everything else in this trace.
tool.executed event from agent-tools. The file path and byte count go into metadata (visible if you trust the agent) and the payload hash (291b1cfc1328…) is what gets signed and chained.

Encode to canonical bytes: 237 bytes. Domain tag PROV_EVENT_V1, schema version 1, length-prefixed strings, metadata sorted by key. Re-encoding the same event always produces these exact bytes — that's what "canonical" means.

Hash the bytes once with SHA3-256, sign the hash with Ed25519, attach the public key. 96 bytes total (32 + 64 + 32) of cryptographic material per event — a one-time fixed cost regardless of payload size.
Chain link: prev_event_hash = 9c8aaa3c248546…, the leaf hash of event 1. The chain advances. Reordering or inserting an event breaks this link — the next event's signature won't match.
Merkle proof: 3 sibling hashes (~96 bytes). Walking those siblings reconstructs the chain root (e38439a06313…). Proof size stays small even when the chain grows large.

Anyone with the agent's public key (2d6e3d8280f2…) and the chain root (e38439a06313…) can verify: signature verifies ✓, chain link links cleanly ✓, Merkle proof verifies against the chain root ✓. Three independent checks, all local, all crypto. No "audit service" in the loop to trust.

records.summary: The agent wrote a file. Real Ed25519 keypair, real SHA3-256 digest, real Merkle path against the chain root.
records.aftermath: The output file's content hash is included in the payload hash, so anyone with the file can verify the agent produced this exact bytes — not a modified version. Hot tier holds the event for ~7 days before rotating to warm storage; the Merkle root persists indefinitely.

beats.copy: The agent wrote a file — event 3 of 5 in this trace. Writes are higher-stakes than reads, and they still get one signed event, one chain link, one Merkle leaf. Same cost. Same proof.
Same agent (agent-tools), different tool. The chain doesn't care whether you read or write — each tool call gets one signed event with its own payload hash, metadata, and Merkle leaf.

Encode to canonical bytes: 234 bytes. The first ~32 bytes match the previous event (same domain tag + schema version + agent_id length-prefix), which is correct and deterministic — it's not a copy-paste. The rest of the bytes diverge based on event_type, payload, and metadata.

The signature here (6b3f5d685cca42…) is a real Ed25519 signature, not a hash truncation or a placeholder. Verifiable with curve25519 implementations in any language — including the one running in your browser.
Chain link: prev_event_hash = 9129fc5936865e…, the leaf hash of event 2. The chain advances. Reordering or inserting an event breaks this link — the next event's signature won't match.
Merkle proof: 3 sibling hashes (~96 bytes). Walking those siblings reconstructs the chain root (e38439a06313…). Proof size stays small even when the chain grows large.

Anyone with the agent's public key (2d6e3d8280f2…) and the chain root (e38439a06313…) can verify: signature verifies ✓, chain link links cleanly ✓, Merkle proof verifies against the chain root ✓. Three independent checks, all local, all crypto. No "audit service" in the loop to trust.

records.summary: A tool call failed. Real Ed25519 keypair, real SHA3-256 digest, real Merkle path against the chain root.
records.aftermath: Failures are preserved in the chain with their error trace — the orchestrator can later replay the failed call to debug or analyze retry patterns. Cold tier archives keep failure events alongside successes; there's no separate "good events" log to selectively trust.

beats.copy: A tool call failed — event 4 of 5 in this trace. Failures get the same audit treatment as successes. Every event is logged regardless of outcome, so an after-the-fact narrative can't quietly omit the parts that went wrong.
tool.failed — yes, failures get the full treatment too. The error_code and timeout duration are in the metadata, signed alongside everything else. No "happy path" log to selectively trust.

Encode to canonical bytes: 252 bytes. The first ~32 bytes match the previous event (same domain tag + schema version + agent_id length-prefix), which is correct and deterministic — it's not a copy-paste. The rest of the bytes diverge based on event_type, payload, and metadata.

Even though this tool call failed, the failure event gets the same SHA3-256 + Ed25519 + public-key bundle. Failures are first-class citizens of the chain; you can't selectively skip signing the embarrassing ones.
Chain link: prev_event_hash = c08c0af1017b81…, the leaf hash of event 3. The chain advances. Reordering or inserting an event breaks this link — the next event's signature won't match.
Merkle proof: 3 sibling hashes (~96 bytes). Walking those siblings reconstructs the chain root (e38439a06313…). Proof size stays small even when the chain grows large.

Anyone with the agent's public key (2d6e3d8280f2…) and the chain root (e38439a06313…) can verify: signature verifies ✓, chain link links cleanly ✓, Merkle proof verifies against the chain root ✓. Three independent checks, all local, all crypto. No "audit service" in the loop to trust.

records.plain: the agent despawned — then someone tampered with the record
records.summary: Signed honestly, then tampered with after the fact. The signature exposes the lie — anyone can verify this independently, with no audit service in the loop.
records.aftermath: The orchestrator's auditor flags the event on the next verify pass. The trace is preserved with the tamper recorded — the chain doesn't censor failed verifications, it just makes them undeniable. Downstream systems can decide what to do; the cryptographic fact is unambiguous.

beats.copy: The agent despawned — then someone tampered with the record — event 5 of 5 in this trace. This event was signed honestly. Then we mutated the metadata after the fact (someone trying to inflate the activity log). Signature verification catches it: the signature was over the original bytes, and the bytes don't match anymore.

agent.despawn from the orchestrator. The metadata claims 3 tools ran (we'll come back to that). Payload hash 38554786e330… commits to the original event bytes — the signature lives or dies on whether those bytes change.

Encode to canonical bytes: 209 bytes. Domain tag PROV_EVENT_V1, schema version 1, length-prefixed strings, metadata sorted by key. Re-encoding the same event always produces these exact bytes — that's what "canonical" means.

Ed25519 signs over the original canonical bytes (before any tampering). That signature commits to a specific byte sequence — changing any field after the fact invalidates the signature even though the public key and the chain link both still match.
Chain link: prev_event_hash = fb5791691eac3d…, the leaf hash of event 4. The chain advances. Reordering or inserting an event breaks this link — the next event's signature won't match.

Merkle proof: 3 sibling hashes (~96 bytes). 2 of the levels had an odd number of nodes, so the last entry is paired with itself (the "duplicate-last-for-odd" rule). The verifier walks each level from leaf to root using these siblings — proof size stays small even when the chain grows large.

This event was signed honestly. Then someone changed "tools_run" from "3" to "30" after the fact. The signature was made over the original bytes — the new bytes are not what got signed. Result: signature FAILS to verify ✗. The Merkle proof and chain link still pass, because they prove this event sits in this position of the chain — not what the event contained. The signature is what catches the content tamper, and three independent checks means one failure is enough.
